Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time each day, but between work and everything else going on, I'd end up cramming sessions into whatever gaps I could find. I spent around 35 hours a week studying, but it felt scattered rather than organized. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to watch videos and make diagrams, but without reliable internet at home, planning was difficult. Most of my 'routine' was really just reacting to whatever was most urgent.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in regularly, but other weeks I'd barely touch anything until an assignment was due. The anxiety would build up and then I'd have these intense study bursts. I wish I could say I had regular times, but life kept getting in the way. Deadlines were really what drove most of my activity.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline or realizing I'd fallen behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new I'd missed, but that was less common. When I was feeling particularly low, even logging in felt like a huge effort. The notification emails would pile up and eventually guilt would push me to check in. It wasn't really a proactive thing for me.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became somewhat routine, maybe during the first few weeks of the course when my motivation was higher. But it never really stuck. The mindfulness exercises they suggested actually helped a bit with establishing some consistency, but my anxiety would spike and throw everything off again. Without internet at home, I had to plan trips to campus or cafes, which made routine difficult.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The discussion forums were helpful too when other students explained concepts in different ways. I watched quite a bit of video content when I could access it. Anything that let me see the material rather than just read it made a real difference for my understanding.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. Long text documents just don't stick in my brain the same way visual content does. I'd delay discussion posts because writing out my thoughts when I'm feeling sad and anxious is exhausting. Some resources I'd open with good intentions but then close without really engaging. The supplementary readings especially felt like too much when I was already struggling to keep up with the core material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams. If it helped me visualize a concept or would clearly boost my grade, I'd engage with it. When I was already anxious about falling behind, anything that felt like extra work without clear payoff got pushed aside. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me recognize when something was genuinely useful versus when I was just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones had deadlines attached, so they got done even if poorly. With optional stuff, my brain would say 'you're already drowning, don't add more.' There was guilt about skipping them because I knew they'd help, but my low resilience meant I had to conserve energy. Required activities at least gave me structure when everything else felt chaotic.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had internet access, then study them offline at home for hours. I made handwritten notes and diagrams that I'd review repeatedly. I also used some YouTube videos and external resources that explained concepts visually. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials spread across my desk at home, completely invisible to the LMS.
Q10: Many times I'd download a lecture, watch it offline multiple times, pause to draw diagrams, and really work through the concepts over several days. The LMS would just show one download or one video view. I'd also study with a classmate sometimes, working through problems together using printed materials. My best learning often happened when I was away from the computer entirely, just working with my notes.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach given my internet situation. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them offline for days. A single login might fuel a week of studying. I'd annotate PDFs, create visual summaries, and review video content multiple times. The LMS data would show minimal activity while I was actually putting in serious hours.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. When my anxiety was peaking, even thinking about the course would make me feel sick. Mid-semester was particularly hard when assignments piled up and I felt like I was drowning. The sadness would settle in and I'd isolate myself, which made everything worse. Sometimes competing responsibilities with work or family just consumed all my energy.
Q13: All of those factors affected me constantly. Fatigue from anxiety and poor sleep made it hard to concentrate. Confusion about course material would spiral into more anxiety, creating this awful cycle. I work part-time with limited resources, so sometimes choosing between studying and earning money was a real conflict. The LMS became associated with stress, which made me avoid it even when I knew I shouldn't.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos or readings while my mind was completely elsewhere, consumed by worry. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing anything. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. My eyes would be on the screen but my brain was stuck in anxious loops about deadlines or life stuff.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel overwhelming, so I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. Discussion posts especially got delayed because putting my thoughts out there felt vulnerable when I was already feeling low. I knew this pattern was hurting me, but knowing and changing are different things. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit with recognizing this avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have low activity for weeks, then intense cramming sessions right before something was due. The LMS would show these spikes of activity clustered around due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have completed anything because the anxiety about starting was so paralyzing.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a brief boost, but negative or critical feedback would send me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my limited energy. But often I'd read feedback once and then not return to it because revisiting felt painful.
Q18: The course structure with its regular deadlines gave me external scaffolding I desperately needed. But the pace sometimes felt relentless when I was struggling. Having five online courses simultaneously was overwhelming. The structure helped when I could keep up, but when I fell behind, it became another source of anxiety. I needed more flexibility than the structure allowed.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for someone like me. But the heavy reliance on discussion forums was harder because writing takes so much energy when you're anxious and sad. The lack of flexibility around deadlines made things harder during rough patches. Having materials downloadable was crucial given my internet situation. More visual summaries and fewer text-heavy readings would have helped me.
Q20: I think that could be someone desperately trying to engage but struggling to produce. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, trying to understand, but the anxiety or confusion prevents them from completing work. It could also be someone like me who downloads everything but doesn't submit because the final step of turning something in feels too vulnerable. High activity doesn't mean high output when you're struggling mentally.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system. My exam score was decent despite low engagement metrics because I was actually studying, just not in visible ways. Someone might have strong external resources, study groups, or just efficient learning strategies that don't require constant LMS presence. The system can't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you access, and deadline-driven behavior. They're good at tracking clicks and time online. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and real understanding. They can't tell if someone staring at a video is learning or dissociating from anxiety. They miss the human context entirely.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when your mind is present and curious, not just going through motions. For me, real engagement happens when I'm making connections, creating visual maps, discussing ideas with others. It's not about how many times I log in, but whether I'm genuinely learning and growing. Engagement should feel meaningful, not just measurable.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope, or how many hours I spent with downloaded materials at home. It wouldn't capture the sadness and anxiety that colored every interaction with the course. It can't see that my 'low engagement' was actually me fighting hard just to stay afloat. The human struggle behind the numbers is completely invisible, and that's probably the most important part of my story.
